Dedication 
To the One who sought me, saved me, and still walks with me, Jesus 
Christ,
 
my
 
Savior
 
and
 
Lord.
 
And to every weary seeker, silent doubter, and honest questioner, may you 
find,
 
as
 
I
 
did,
 
that
 
faith
 
in
 
Christ
 
is
 
not
 
the
 
end
 
of
 
your
 
questions,
 
but
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
true
 
answers.
 
3

Acknowledgements 
To the Great Haven Assembly family, thank you for being a people who 
embrace
 
truth,
 
pursue
 
the
 
presence
 
of
 
God,
 
and
 
love
 
the
 
Church
 
well.
 
Your
 
hunger
 
for
 
God's
 
Word
 
and
 
passion
 
for
 
authentic
 
faith
 
continue
 
to
 
inspire
 
me.
 
To the skeptics, searchers, and saints alike, this is for you. May this humble 
effort
 
help
 
strengthen
 
your
 
faith
 
or
 
stir
 
your
 
heart
 
again
 
toward
 
Jesus.
 
 
 
4

INTRODUCTION 
 
Life has a way of leading us to the quiet corners of our hearts, where 
questions
 
begin
 
to
 
rise,
 
questions
 
that
 
no
 
social
 
media
 
scroll,
 
weekend
 
getaway,
 
or
 
intellectual
 
distraction
 
can
 
fully
 
silence.
 
In
 
these
 
moments,
 
we
 
find
 
ourselves
 
wondering:
 
● Why am I here? 
● Is there more to life than this? 
● If there is a God, where is He in all of this mess? 
● Can anything truly make sense of this broken, beautiful world? 
If you’ve ever wrestled with these questions, you’re not alone. 
I didn’t come to faith in Christ by pretending my doubts didn’t exist. In 
fact,
 
it
 
was
 
through
 
grappling
 
with
 
these
 
doubts
 
that
 
I
 
encountered
 
the
 
beauty
 
and
 
truth
 
of
 
the
 
gospel.
 
For
 
many
 
of
 
us,
 
faith
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
linear
 
journey
 
but
 
a
 
winding
 
path,
 
a
 
journey
 
that
 
often
 
feels
 
more
 
like
 
a
 
dance
 
of
 
curiosity,
 
contradiction,
 
pain,
 
and
 
moments
 
of
 
wonder.
 
We live in a time where many are rethinking what they believe. For some, 
it’s
 
called
 
deconstruction;
 
for
 
others,
 
it’s
 
a
 
crisis
 
of
 
faith.
 
Whether
 
it’s
 
through
 
trauma,
 
church
 
hurt,
 
academic
 
pressures,
 
cultural
 
confusion,
 
or
 
personal
 
suffering,
 
many
 
find
 
themselves
 
questioning
 
the
 
story
 
they
 
were
 
told.
 
If
 
that
 
resonates
 
with
 
you,
 
I
 
want
 
to
 
assure
 
you:
 
asking
 
hard
 
questions
 
is
 
not
 
the
 
enemy
 
of
 
faith.
 
In
 
fact,
 
those
 
questions,
 
when
 
asked
 
with
 
an
 
open
 
heart,
 
can
 
be
 
the
 
very
 
beginning
 
of
 
a
 
deeper,
 
more
 
meaningful
 
walk
 
with
 
God.
 
 7

Why This Book Was Written 
This book is not about winning an argument or providing a neat 
conclusion.
 
It’s
 
an
 
invitation
 
to
 
pause,
 
to
 
listen,
 
and
 
to
 
reflect.
 
It’s
 
an
 
invitation
 
to
 
explore
 
why
 
Christianity
 
still
 
matters,
 
not
 
just
 
historically
 
or
 
religiously,
 
but
 
personally,
 
in
 
the
 
deepest
 
places
 
of
 
our
 
hearts.
 
Why I Am a Christian is for the seeker who longs for something more 
than
 
clichés,
 
for
 
the
 
believer
 
who’s
 
barely
 
hanging
 
on,
 
and
 
for
 
the
 
skeptic
 
who
 
still
 
feels
 
the
 
pull
 
of
 
eternity
 
deep
 
within.
 
It’s
 
for
 
those
 
who
 
are
 
tired
 
of
 
shallow
 
answers
 
and
 
superficial
 
faith.
 
We believe that Christianity is not just a religion, it is a worldview that 
aligns
 
with
 
the
 
very
 
fabric
 
of
 
reality.
 
It
 
speaks
 
to
 
the
 
depth
 
of
 
the
 
human
 
condition
 
and
 
provides
 
answers
 
to
 
life’s
 
most
 
pressing
 
questions
 
with
 
clarity,
 
compassion,
 
and
 
truth.
 
Christianity
 
does
 
not
 
ask
 
you
 
to
 
shut
 
off
 
your
 
mind,
 
but
 
to
 
open
 
your
 
heart.
 
It
 
does
 
not
 
ignore
 
the
 
brokenness
 
of
 
the
 
world,
 
but
 
rather
 
explains
 
it
 
and
 
shows
 
us
 
what
 
God
 
has
 
done
 
to
 
redeem
 
it.
 
If you’ve ever felt your soul stirred by beauty, moved by justice, 
overwhelmed
 
by
 
pain,
 
or
 
awakened
 
by
 
love,
 
you
 
are
 
already
 
on
 
the
 
edge
 
of
 
something
 
sacred.
 
The
 
Christian
 
faith
 
doesn’t
 
deny
 
these
 
experiences;
 
it
 
explains
 
them.
 
In
 
fact,
 
it
 
is
 
in
 
these
 
very
 
experiences
 
that
 
we
 
find
 
the
 
deepest
 
resonance
 
with
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
the
 
gospel.
 
This book is our humble attempt to share what we have seen and 
experienced:
 
that
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ
,
 
we
 
find
 
not
 
just
 
truth,
 
but
 
peace
.
 
Not
 
just
 
meaning,
 
but
 
a
 
Person
.
 
Not
 
just
 
a
 
system
 
of
 
beliefs,
 
but
 
a
 
Savior
.
 
As you read through these pages, my prayer is that your heart will be 
stirred
 
and
 
your
 
questions
 
met
 
with
 
a
 
living
 
hope.
 
I
 
pray
 
that
 
through
 
this
 
journey,
 
you
 
will
 
see
 
not
 
just
 
the
 
answers,
 
but
 
the
 
person
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ,
 
a
 
Savior
 
who
 
is
 
with
 
us
 
in
 
our
 
doubts
 
and
 
in
 
our
 
hopes.
 
8

PREFACE 
 
This book is a product of a deep burden that has grown within me over 
many
 
years
 
of
 
engaging
 
with
 
individuals,
 
both
 
within
 
and
 
outside
 
the
 
church,
 
who,
 
despite
 
having
 
a
 
Christian
 
heritage,
 
have
 
been
 
disillusioned
 
by
 
life’s
 
challenges
 
or
 
the
 
disappointments
 
they
 
have
 
encountered
 
through
 
the
 
church.
 
These
 
people,
 
who
 
once
 
knew
 
the
 
joy
 
and
 
vitality
 
of
 
their
 
faith,
 
now
 
find
 
themselves
 
questioning
 
whether
 
it
 
is
 
still
 
worth
 
pursuing.
 
The
 
wounds
 
of
 
unmet
 
expectations,
 
the
 
scars
 
of
 
church
 
hurt,
 
and
 
the
 
increasing
 
wave
 
of
 
deconstruction
 
that
 
has
 
swept
 
through
 
many
 
believers’
 
lives
 
have
 
left
 
them
 
in
 
a
 
place
 
of
 
spiritual
 
uncertainty.
 
In many conversations, I have heard the resounding cry of skepticism and 
doubt.
 
The
 
very
 
faith
 
that
 
once
 
brought
 
life
 
and
 
purpose
 
seems
 
distant,
 
and
 
for
 
many,
 
the
 
church
 
feels
 
like
 
a
 
place
 
of
 
failure
 
rather
 
than
 
a
 
sanctuary.
 
Yet,
 
through
 
these
 
dialogues,
 
something
 
beautiful
 
has
 
emerged,
 
a
 
strong
 
conviction
 
in
 
my
 
heart
 
that
 
the
 
church,
 
our
 
church
,
 
is
 
called
 
to
 
be
 
a
 
place
 
of
 
healing,
 
restoration,
 
and
 
renewal.
 
We
 
are
 
called
 
to
 
be
 
a
 
church
 
to
 
the
 
skeptics,
 
to
 
those
 
who
 
have
 
wandered,
 
to
 
those
 
who
 
have
 
been
 
wounded.
 
We
 
are
 
here
 
to
 
help
 
them
 
regain
 
and
 
reconstruct
 
their
 
faith
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
and
 
to
 
see
 
the
 
vibrancy
 
and
 
fervency
 
of
 
that
 
faith
 
reignited
 
in
 
their
 
lives.
 
It is with this passion and this purpose that I wrote this book. As believers, 
we
 
are
 
part
 
of
 
a
 
community
 
that
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
about
 
gathering
 
on
 
Sundays
 
or
 
participating
 
in
 
religious
 
activities;
 
it
 
is
 
about
 
living
 
out
 
a
 
transformative,
 
vibrant
 
faith
 
in
 
Christ,
 
one
 
that
 
sustains
 
us
 
through
 
trials,
 
doubts,
 
and
 
the
 
darkest
 
seasons
 
of
 
life.
 
It
 
is
 
my
 
prayer
 
that,
 
through
 
this
 
volume,
 
you
 
will
 
find
 
the
 
support
 
and
 
encouragement
 
you
 
need
 
when
 
doubt
 
creeps
 
in,
 
when
 
the
 
absence
 
of
 
a
 
pastor
 
or
 
Christian
 
companionship
 
feels
 
overwhelming.
 
The
 
journey
 
of
 
faith
 
can
 
sometimes
 
feel
 
like
 
a
 
solitary
 
walk,
 
but
 
I
 
believe
 
9

that through the principles shared in these pages, you will encounter the 
love,
 
grace,
 
and
 
presence
 
of
 
Jesus.
 
Church is not a social gathering or a mere club house, and I want to make 
that
 
very
 
clear
 
from
 
the
 
outset.
 
While
 
the
 
cozy
 
atmosphere,
 
beautiful
 
music,
 
and
 
welcoming
 
faces
 
are
 
important,
 
they
 
are
 
not
 
the
 
essence
 
of
 
what
 
it
 
means
 
to
 
be
 
the
 
church.
 
The
 
church
 
is
 
the
 
living,
 
breathing
 
body
 
of
 
Christ,
 
a
 
vibrant
 
fellowship
 
of
 
saints
 
committed
 
to
 
each
 
other,
 
to
 
the
 
world,
 
and
 
to
 
the
 
mission
 
of
 
God.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
community
 
that
 
is
 
meant
 
to
 
bring
 
life,
 
not
 
just
 
on
 
Sundays,
 
but
 
every
 
single
 
day.
 
I pray that as you read this book, your heart will open more to Jesus. May 
you
 
experience
 
an
 
encounter
 
with
 
Him
 
that
 
revives
 
your
 
soul,
 
restores
 
your
 
hope,
 
and
 
strengthens
 
your
 
faith.
 
As
 
you
 
walk
 
through
 
the
 
pages,
 
may
 
your
 
heart
 
be
 
touched
 
by
 
the
 
One
 
who
 
is
 
always
 
near,
 
even
 
when
 
it
 
feels
 
like
 
He
 
is
 
far
 
away.
 
This
 
book
 
is
 
a
 
call
 
to
 
return,
 
to
 
rebuild,
 
to
 
rekindle
 
the
 
fire
 
of
 
your
 
first
 
love
 
with
 
Christ.
 
May this work be a companion on your journey, a support for the weary, a 
guide
 
for
 
the
 
uncertain,
 
and
 
an
 
invitation
 
to
 
experience
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
life
 
in
 
Christ.
 
When
 
doubts
 
arise,
 
when
 
the
 
road
 
gets
 
rough,
 
remember
 
that
 
Jesus
 
is
 
still
 
the
 
answer,
 
and
 
He
 
is
 
always
 
ready
 
to
 
meet
 
you
 
where
 
you
 
are.
 
Welcome back to the heart of your faith. Welcome back to the church. 
Welcome
 
back
 
to
 
the
 
vibrancy
 
and
 
beauty
 
of
 
life
 
in
 
Christ.
 
 
 
 
 
10

PART I: FOUNDATIONS OF REALITY
11

CHAPTER 1 
CHRISTIANITY AND THE QUESTIONS THAT HAUNT US ALL 
 
WHO AM I? 
The question of identity is not a modern obsession, it is a timeless human 
cry.
 
Beneath
 
our
 
pursuit
 
of
 
success,
 
affirmation,
 
and
 
belonging
 
lies
 
this
 
deep,
 
often
 
unspoken
 
question:
 
Who
 
am
 
I,
 
really?
 
In today’s culture, identity has become increasingly fluid and 
self-referential.
 
Modern
 
secularism
 
encourages
 
us
 
to
 
look
 
inward
 
to
 
define
 
ourselves:
 
“You
 
are
 
who
 
you
 
feel
 
you
 
are.
 
Define
 
your
 
own
 
truth.
 
Construct
 
your
 
own
 
meaning.”
 
While
 
this
 
appears
 
liberating
 
on
 
the
 
surface,
 
it
 
ultimately
 
places
 
an
 
unbearable
 
weight
 
on
 
the
 
individual.
 
What
 
happens
 
when
 
our
 
self-perception
 
breaks
 
down?
 
When
 
our
 
feelings
 
shift?
 
When
 
life
 
doesn’t
 
reflect
 
the
 
story
 
we
 
tried
 
to
 
write
 
for
 
ourselves?
 
Evolutionary naturalism, one of the dominant narratives in postmodern 
thought,
 
tells
 
us
 
we
 
are
 
the
 
result
 
of
 
blind
 
processes:
 
time,
 
matter,
 
and
 
chance.
 
According
 
to
 
this
 
view,
 
you
 
and
 
I
 
are
 
essentially
 
highly-evolved
 
animals,
 
products
 
of
 
genetic
 
mutations
 
optimized
 
for
 
survival.
 
Consciousness,
 
emotion,
 
morality,
 
and
 
love,
 
these
 
are
 
seen
 
as
 
evolutionary
 
byproducts,
 
useful
 
illusions
 
that
 
help
 
us
 
cope
 
and
 
cooperate.
 
But such a view struggles to explain the things we most deeply value. Why 
do
 
we
 
instinctively
 
long
 
for
 
justice,
 
beauty,
 
meaning,
 
and
 
love?
 
Why
 
do
 
we
 
grieve
 
loss
 
so
 
deeply,
 
if
 
human
 
life
 
is
 
just
 
another
 
blip
 
in
 
the
 
evolutionary
 
timeline?
 
Why
 
do
 
we
 
ask
 
about
 
purpose
 
at
 
all?
 
12

The Christian Answer: Imago Dei 
Christianity stands in stark contrast by proclaiming a bold and beautiful 
truth:
 
You
 
are
 
made
 
in
 
the
 
image
 
of
 
God
 
(Latin:
 
Imago
 
Dei
),
 
Genesis
 
1:26-27.
 
“And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our 
likeness...”
 
(Genesis
 
1:26,
 
KJV)
 
To be made in God’s image means that you are not an accident. You are not 
a
 
random
 
collection
 
of
 
molecules,
 
nor
 
are
 
you
 
defined
 
by
 
your
 
performance
 
or
 
appearance.
 
Your
 
identity
 
is
 
rooted
 
in
 
something
 
(and
 
Someone)
 
far
 
more
 
secure:
 
you
 
are
 
created
 
by
 
a
 
personal,
 
intentional,
 
and
 
loving
 
God.
 
This biblical truth bestows upon every human being inherent dignity, moral 
responsibility,
 
and
 
relational
 
capacity.
 
You
 
were
 
designed
 
to
 
reflect
 
aspects
 
of
 
God’s
 
nature,
 
creativity,
 
rationality,
 
compassion,
 
authority,
 
and
 
holiness.
 
Theologians
 
describe
 
the
 
Imago
 
Dei
 
as
 
the
 
foundation
 
for
 
human
 
rights
 
and
 
ethical
 
responsibility.
 
Without
 
it,
 
any
 
claim
 
to
 
human
 
worth
 
becomes
 
arbitrary.
 
Even in your brokenness, the image remains. It may be marred by sin, but 
it
 
is
 
not
 
erased.
 
And
 
here
 
is
 
the
 
Christian
 
paradox:
 
even
 
though
 
we
 
have
 
rebelled
 
against
 
the
 
One
 
who
 
made
 
us,
 
God
 
did
 
not
 
abandon
 
us.
 
He
 
sent
 
His
 
Son,
 
Jesus
 
Christ,
 
the
 
perfect
 
image
 
of
 
God
 
(Hebrews
 
1:3;
 
Colossians
 
1:15),
 
to
 
restore
 
what
 
was
 
lost
 
and
 
reconcile
 
us
 
to
 
Himself.
 
Beyond Self-Esteem: Toward Sacred Identity 
The cultural push for self-esteem, while well-meaning, is ultimately hollow 
when
 
detached
 
from
 
divine
 
identity.
 
Secular
 
messages
 
say,
 
“You
 
are
 
enough.”
 
But
 
what
 
if
 
I
 
don’t
 
feel
 
like
 
I’m
 
enough?
 
What
 
if
 
my
 
failures,
 
wounds,
 
or
 
traumas
 
run
 
deeper
 
than
 
affirmation
 
can
 
reach?
 13

The Christian gospel doesn’t prop up our self-worth with slogans. It does 
something
 
better,
 
it
 
roots
 
our
 
worth
 
in
 
the
 
unchanging
 
character
 
of
 
God.
 
In
 
Christ,
 
your
 
value
 
is
 
not
 
based
 
on
 
what
 
you
 
do,
 
how
 
you
 
feel,
 
or
 
what
 
others
 
say
 
about
 
you.
 
It
 
is
 
based
 
on
 
the
 
fact
 
that
 
you
 
are
 
known,
 
loved,
 
and
 
chosen
 
by
 
the
 
God
 
who
 
made
 
you.
 
“Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee...” (Jeremiah 
1:5)
 
 
“Ye
 
are
 
bought
 
with
 
a
 
price...”
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
6:20)
 
This is not self-help, it is sacred help. You are not merely a body; you are a 
soul.
 
Not
 
a
 
cosmic
 
accident,
 
but
 
a
 
divine
 
intention.
 
Not
 
a
 
statistic,
 
but
 
a
 
story
 
that
 
God
 
is
 
writing
 
for
 
His
 
glory.
 
A Name, Not Just a Number 
In a digital age where people are reduced to data points, usernames, or 
followers,
 
the
 
gospel
 
reminds
 
us:
 
you
 
are
 
not
 
forgotten.
 
You
 
are
 
not
 
just
 
one
 
of
 
billions.
 
You
 
are
 
not
 
just
 
“someone.”
 
You
 
are
 
someone
 
to
 
God
.
 
“Fear not: for I have redeemed thee, I have called thee by thy 
name;
 
thou
 
art
 
mine.”
 
(Isaiah
 
43:1)
 
To know Christ is to be fully known, and fully loved. Not for a version of 
you
 
that
 
performs,
 
pleases,
 
or
 
pretends,
 
but
 
for
 
you
.
 
The
 
real
 
you.
 
And
 
the
 
more
 
you
 
grow
 
in
 
your
 
relationship
 
with
 
Jesus,
 
the
 
more
 
clearly
 
you
 
begin
 
to
 
see
 
who
 
you
 
really
 
are.
 
The True Mirror 
The philosopher Søren Kierkegaard once wrote that the self “must be 
grounded
 
transparently
 
in
 
the
 
Power
 
which
 
established
 
it.”
 
In
 
other
 
words,
 
to
 
know
 
who
 
you
 
are,
 
you
 
must
 
look
 
beyond
 
yourself,
 
to
 
the
 
One
 
who
 
made
 
you.
 
14

Jesus is that mirror. He shows us not only who God is, but who we are 
meant
 
to
 
be.
 
And
 
in
 
Him,
 
the
 
question
 
“Who
 
am
 
I?”
 
is
 
answered
 
not
 
with
 
confusion
 
or
 
despair,
 
but
 
with
 
hope,
 
truth,
 
and
 
grace.
 
WHY IS THERE SUFFERING? 
One of the most piercing and personal questions every human being must 
wrestle
 
with
 
is:
 
If
 
God
 
is
 
good
 
and
 
powerful,
 
why
 
does
 
He
 
allow
 
suffering?
 
This question is not merely philosophical, it is deeply emotional. Whether 
it’s
 
the
 
ache
 
of
 
losing
 
a
 
loved
 
one,
 
the
 
terror
 
of
 
war,
 
the
 
sting
 
of
 
betrayal,
 
or
 
the
 
chronic
 
pain
 
of
 
a
 
body
 
breaking
 
down,
 
suffering
 
reaches
 
us
 
all.
 
No
 
one
 
is
 
immune.
 
Secular responses to suffering often fall short. Naturalism, for instance, 
tells
 
us
 
that
 
suffering
 
is
 
merely
 
the
 
byproduct
 
of
 
a
 
blind,
 
impersonal
 
universe
 
governed
 
by
 
chance.
 
According
 
to
 
this
 
view,
 
there
 
is
 
no
 
inherent
 
purpose
 
in
 
your
 
pain,
 
only
 
biology
 
and
 
bad
 
luck.
 
Such
 
a
 
perspective
 
may
 
attempt
 
to
 
be
 
intellectually
 
consistent,
 
but
 
it
 
offers
 
no
 
comfort,
 
no
 
moral
 
framework,
 
and
 
no
 
hope.
 
It
 
reduces
 
the
 
cry
 
of
 
a
 
grieving
 
mother
 
to
 
meaningless
 
chemical
 
reactions
 
in
 
the
 
brain.
 
Other worldviews try to avoid the problem by denying suffering altogether. 
In
 
some
 
strands
 
of
 
Eastern
 
philosophy,
 
pain
 
is
 
said
 
to
 
be
 
an
 
illusion,
 
something
 
to
 
be
 
transcended
 
through
 
detachment
 
or
 
spiritual
 
awakening.
 
But
 
to
 
say
 
that
 
pain
 
is
 
an
 
illusion
 
is
 
to
 
dismiss
 
the
 
real
 
anguish
 
people
 
endure.
 
This
 
denial
 
may
 
soothe
 
for
 
a
 
moment,
 
but
 
it
 
ultimately
 
demands
 
we
 
shut
 
down
 
our
 
humanity.
 
Still others blame social systems or karma, suggesting that all suffering is 
either
 
deserved
 
or
 
simply
 
a
 
tool
 
for
 
balancing
 
out
 
the
 
universe.
 
But
 
this
 
leads
 
to
 
another
 
injustice:
 
blaming
 
victims
 
for
 
their
 
suffering
 
and
 
offering
 
no
 
hope
 
for
 
redemption,
 
only
 
an
 
endless
 
cycle
 
of
 
cause
 
and
 
effect.
 
15

The Christian Response: A Broken World, A Present God 
Christianity does not sidestep the problem of suffering. It faces it head-on. 
According
 
to
 
Scripture,
 
suffering
 
entered
 
the
 
world
 
not
 
as
 
part
 
of
 
God's
 
original
 
design,
 
but
 
as
 
a
 
consequence
 
of
 
human
 
sin.
 
“Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and 
death
 
by
 
sin...”
 
(Romans
 
5:12,
 
KJV)
 
Sin fractured the harmony of creation. It severed humanity’s relationship 
with
 
God,
 
with
 
each
 
other,
 
and
 
even
 
with
 
creation
 
itself
 
(Genesis
 
3).
 
From
 
that
 
rupture
 
flowed
 
every
 
kind
 
of
 
pain,
 
violence,
 
disease,
 
natural
 
disasters,
 
oppression,
 
loneliness,
 
and
 
death.
 
The
 
Bible
 
reveals
 
that
 
suffering
 
is
 
not
 
random.
 
It
 
has
 
a
 
cause,
 
and
 
it
 
has
 
a
 
Redeemer.
 
But here's the staggering claim of Christianity: God did not remain 
distant
 
from
 
our
 
pain.
 
He
 
entered
 
it.
 
Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh and lived in our 
world
 
of
 
suffering.
 
He
 
did
 
not
 
merely
 
observe
 
our
 
brokenness
 
from
 
afar;
 
He
 
walked
 
in
 
it,
 
wept
 
in
 
it,
 
and
 
ultimately
 
bore
 
it.
 
“Surely he hath borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows...” 
(Isaiah
 
53:4)
 
 
“For
 
we
 
have
 
not
 
an
 
high
 
priest
 
which
 
cannot
 
be
 
touched
 
with
 
the
 
feeling
 
of
 
our
 
infirmities...”
 
(Hebrews
 
4:15)
 
At the cross, Jesus endured the full weight of human evil and divine 
justice.
 
He
 
suffered
 
not
 
only
 
physical
 
agony,
 
but
 
the
 
spiritual
 
anguish
 
of
 
taking
 
upon
 
Himself
 
the
 
sins
 
of
 
the
 
world
 
(2
 
Corinthians
 
5:21).
 
In
 
doing
 
so,
 
He
 
did
 
what
 
no
 
other
 
religious
 
figure
 
even
 
claims
 
to
 
have
 
done:
 
He
 
suffered
 
with
 
us
 
and
 
for
 
us.
 
This means two things for the believer:

The Reliability of the New Testament 
The New Testament documents are the most well-attested texts from the 
ancient
 
world
 
by
 
a
 
significant
 
margin.
 
With
 
over
 
5,800
 
Greek
 
manuscripts
,

more
 
in
 
Latin
 
and
 
other
 
languages,
 
and
 
a
 
chain
 
of
 
patristic
 
citations
 
that
 
allow
 
for
 
near-total
 
reconstruction
 
of
 
the
 
New
 
Testament
 
by
 
the
 
fourth
 
century,
 
we
 
possess
 
a
 
textual
 
foundation
 
unparalleled
 
in
 
ancient
 
literature.
 
By
 
comparison,
 
works
 
like
 
Homer’s
 
Iliad
 
or
 
Caesar’s
 
Gallic
 
Wars
 
survive
 
in
 
far
 
fewer
 
and
 
much
 
later
 
copies.
 
The process by which the New Testament canon was recognized was not a 
haphazard
 
vote
 
or
 
political
 
conspiracy,
 
as
 
some
 
popular
 
narratives
 
(e.g.,
 
The
 
Da
 
Vinci
 
Code
)
 
have
 
suggested.
 
Rather,
 
early
 
Christians
 
affirmed
 
texts
 
that
 
were
 
apostolic
 
in
 
origin,
 
orthodox
 
in
 
doctrine,
 
and
 
consistently
 
used
 
in
 
worship
 
across
 
diverse
 
geographical
 
regions.
 
The
 
Muratorian
 
Fragment
 
(c.
 
AD
 
170),
 
the

of
 
Church

like
 
Irenaeus,
 
and
 
the
 
deliberations
 
of
 
church
 
councils
 
reflect
 
the
 
discernment
 
of
 
a
 
community
 
already
 
guided
 
by
 
the
 
Spirit
 
and
 
united
 
by
 
the
 
gospel’s
 
core
 
content.
 
Moreover, the content of the New Testament arose far too early to be 
legendary
 
accretion.
 
Scholars
 
across
 
the
 
spectrum
 
acknowledge
 
that
 
key
 
traditions
 
about
 
Jesus,
 
His
 
death,
 
burial,
 
resurrection,
 
and
 
post-resurrection

were
 
circulating
 
within
 
years,
 
if
 
not
 
months,
 
of
 
the
 
crucifixion.
 
For
 
example,
 
the
 
creed
 
cited
 
by
 
Paul
 
in

Corinthians
 
15:3-8,
 
which
 
he
 
“delivered”
 
but
 
also
 
“received,”

to

5
 
years
 
of

This

same

has

Christ

for

was

and
 
rose
 
again
 
on
 
the
 
third
 
day.
 
This early testimony, combined with the transformation of cowardly

into

witnesses
 
(Acts

of

like

the
 
brother
 
of

(later

persecuted

affirms

that the resurrection was not a later theological embellishment but the

of

The early creeds , such as the Apostles’ Creed and the Nicene Creed, were

The ecumenical councils of the early Church (e.g., Nicaea, Chalcedon)

to

APPENDICES 
 
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS (FAQ) 
Here are some frequently asked questions to help you better understand 
what
 
it
 
means
 
to
 
belong
 
to
 
a
 
church
 
and
 
how
 
you
 
can
 
grow
 
in
 
your
 
faith:
 
1. Why should I join a church? 
While it’s possible to be a Christian without formally belonging to a 
church,
 
Scripture
 
encourages
 
believers
 
to
 
be
 
part
 
of
 
a
 
community.
 
The
 
church
 
is
 
a
 
place
 
where
 
we
 
can
 
grow
 
spiritually,
 
serve
 
others,
 
and
 
be
 
encouraged
 
in
 
our
 
walk
 
with
 
Christ.
 
The
 
New
 
Testament
 
speaks
 
of
 
believers
 
as
 
members
 
of
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ,
 
where
 
each
 
part
 
is
 
essential
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
12:12-27).
 
Joining
 
a
 
church
 
allows
 
you
 
to
 
experience
 
the
 
benefits
 
of
 
fellowship,
 
teaching,
 
accountability,
 
and
 
service
 
to
 
others.
 
2. What is the church's role in my spiritual growth? 
The church provides various means of growth: 
● Teaching and Preaching : The Word of God is foundational to our 
growth.
 
Through
 
the
 
Bible’s
 
teachings,
 
we
 
learn
 
God’s
 
will,
 
His
 
promises,
 
and
 
His
 
expectations
 
for
 
our
 
lives.
 
● Fellowship : Being with other believers encourages spiritual 
growth.
 
You
 
are
 
not
 
walking
 
your
 
faith
 
journey
 
alone.
 
● Worship : Corporate worship helps align our hearts with God’s 
truth
 
and
 
reminds
 
us
 
of
 
His
 
greatness
 
and
 
goodness.
 
146

● Service : Engaging in service helps believers use their spiritual gifts 
for
 
the
 
benefit
 
of
 
others,
 
which
 
in
 
turn
 
helps
 
develop
 
Christ-like
 
character.
 
3. Can I grow spiritually without going to church? 
While personal devotion and private worship are vital, the Bible teaches 
that
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith
 
is
 
lived
 
out
 
within
 
community.
 
The
 
early
 
church
 
was
 
characterized
 
by
 
communal
 
worship,
 
shared
 
meals,
 
teaching,
 
and
 
fellowship
 
(Acts
 
2:42).
 
Spiritual
 
growth
 
in
 
isolation
 
can
 
be
 
limited,
 
as
 
we
 
need
 
the
 
encouragement,
 
accountability,
 
and
 
support
 
of
 
other
 
believers
 
to
 
mature
 
in
 
our
 
faith.
 
4. What should I expect when I visit a church for the first time? 
When you visit a church, expect to be welcomed warmly and treated as a 
valued
 
guest.
 
Most
 
churches
 
will
 
have
 
friendly
 
greeters
 
who
 
can
 
help
 
you
 
get
 
settled
 
and
 
answer
 
any
 
questions
 
you
 
may
 
have.
 
During
 
the
 
service,
 
you
 
will
 
likely
 
experience
 
worship
 
through
 
singing,
 
prayer,
 
and
 
a
 
sermon
 
that
 
teaches
 
from
 
the
 
Bible.
 
There
 
may
 
also
 
be
 
opportunities
 
for
 
fellowship
 
after
 
the
 
service,
 
such
 
as
 
a
 
coffee
 
hour
 
or
 
small
 
groups,
 
where
 
you
 
can
 
meet
 
others.
 
5. How can I get involved in the church community? 
Getting involved in the church is one of the best ways to grow spiritually 
and
 
build
 
meaningful
 
relationships.
 
Start
 
by
 
attending
 
a
 
service
 
regularly.
 
You
 
can
 
also
 
participate
 
in
 
small
 
groups,
 
Bible
 
studies,
 
volunteer
 
for
 
church
 
events,
 
or
 
join
 
a
 
ministry
 
team.
 
Most
 
churches
 
have
 
ministries
 
for
 
different
 
age
 
groups,
 
interests,
 
and
 
skill
 
sets,
 
where
 
you
 
can
 
serve
 
and
 
make
 
an
 
impact.
 
6. How can I grow in my relationship with God outside of church?

Spiritual growth is a lifelong process, and much of it happens outside of 
church

To

with

The church is a community where you can find support during difficult

Finding the right church involves prayer, reflection, and discerning 
whether

church’s
 
teaching

with
 
Scripture
 
and

its

spiritual
 
growth.

Most churches have various ministries that need volunteers. Consider your

of

10. What does membership in the church entail? 
Membership in the church typically involves committing to the church’s

and

for

Christianity

robust
